title: Prostate Cancer: Screening, Diagnosis, and Management
objectives: Understand PSA screening recommendations and shared decision-making
Interpret Gleason grading and risk stratification
Apply appropriate management strategies by risk group
Recognize treatment complications and side effects

sections.title: Epidemiology
sections.content:
**Most common non-cutaneous cancer in men**

• Lifetime risk: 1 in 8
• Second leading cause of cancer death in men
• Median age at diagnosis: 66 years

**Risk Factors:**
• Age (strongest factor)
• African American race (2x incidence, higher mortality)
• Family history (BRCA1/2, Lynch syndrome)
• High-fat diet (controversial)

sections.title: PSA Screening
sections.content:
**PSA Basics:**
• Prostate-specific, NOT cancer-specific
• Elevated in BPH, prostatitis, instrumentation, ejaculation

**Screening Recommendations (USPSTF/AUA):**
• Age 55-69: Shared decision-making
• Age <55: Only if high risk
• Age >70: Generally not recommended

**PSA Interpretation:**
• Traditional cutoff: 4.0 ng/mL
• PSA density: PSA/prostate volume
• PSA velocity: >0.75 ng/mL/year concerning
• Free/Total PSA: Lower ratio = higher cancer risk

sections.title: Diagnosis & Grading
sections.content:
**Biopsy Indications:**
• Elevated PSA
• Abnormal DRE
• Rising PSA

**Gleason Grading:**
• Primary + Secondary pattern = Gleason Score
• Grade Groups 1-5 (new system)
• Group 1: Gleason 6 (3+3)
• Group 2: Gleason 7 (3+4)
• Group 3: Gleason 7 (4+3)
• Group 4: Gleason 8
• Group 5: Gleason 9-10

**Key Concept:** 3+4 ≠ 4+3 (4+3 is worse)

sections.title: Risk Stratification
sections.content:
**Low Risk:**
• PSA <10, Gleason ≤6, Stage T1-T2a
• Options: Active surveillance, radiation, surgery

**Intermediate Risk:**
• PSA 10-20, Gleason 7, or Stage T2b-T2c
• Options: Radiation ± ADT, surgery

**High Risk:**
• PSA >20, Gleason 8-10, or Stage T3+
• Options: Radiation + long-term ADT, surgery ± adjuvant therapy

**Metastatic:**
• ADT backbone
• Docetaxel, abiraterone, enzalutamide

sections.title: Treatment Options
sections.content:
**Active Surveillance:**
• For low-risk disease
• Regular PSA, DRE, repeat biopsies
• Avoids treatment side effects

**Radical Prostatectomy:**
• Surgical removal of prostate
• Complications: ED (30-70%), incontinence (5-20%)

**Radiation Therapy:**
• EBRT or brachytherapy
• Similar efficacy to surgery for localized disease
• Complications: Bowel/bladder irritation, ED

**Androgen Deprivation Therapy:**
• GnRH agonists (leuprolide) or antagonists
• Anti-androgens (bicalutamide, enzalutamide)
• Side effects: Hot flashes, osteoporosis, metabolic syndrome

sections.title: Follow-up & Recurrence
sections.content:
**Post-Treatment PSA:**
• After prostatectomy: Should be undetectable
• After radiation: Nadir + 2.0 = biochemical recurrence

**Biochemical Recurrence:**
• PSA rises before clinical recurrence
• Imaging workup (PSMA-PET, bone scan)
• Salvage options: Radiation, ADT

**Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer:**
• Progression despite testosterone <50 ng/dL
• Multiple treatment options available
• Multidisciplinary approach

keyTakeaways: Active surveillance is appropriate for low-risk disease
Treatment decisions based on risk stratification

references.citation: AUA Guideline: Clinically Localized Prostate Cancer

reviewQuestions.question: A 62-year-old man has a prostate biopsy showing Gleason 3+4=7 in 2 of 12 cores. PSA is 8 ng/mL. DRE shows no nodules. What is his risk category?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Intermediate risk is defined by PSA 10-20 OR Gleason 7 OR stage T2b-T2c. His Gleason 7 places him in intermediate risk despite PSA <10.
